title: Acute Respiratory Alkalosis
buzzwords: Sudden hyperventilation with rising pH
Perioral and limb paresthesias minutes after onset
Carpopedal spasm from drop in ionized calcium
Tachypnea triggered by pain or fear
PaCO2 drops fast, bicarbonate near normal
No time for kidneys to compensate
Classic in panic attack or acute PE
Uncompensated low PaCO2, high pH on ABG

title: Chronic Respiratory Alkalosis
buzzwords: Persistent hyperventilation over days to weeks
Mildly elevated pH despite ongoing hypocapnia
Serum bicarbonate low with normal anion gap
Kidneys dump bicarbonate to buffer pH
Seen in high-altitude residents and pregnancy
Renal compensation takes hours to days
Near-normal pH with low PaCO2 and low HCO3-
Compensated primary hypocapnia via bicarbonaturia

title: Pulmonary Embolism (Hypoxemic Cause)
buzzwords: Sudden dyspnea with unexplained tachypnea
Pleuritic chest pain, may be absent
Hyperventilation with or without hypoxemia
Potentially fatal cause of acute alkalosis
Recent immobilization or hypercoagulable risk
Elevated D-dimer triggers further imaging
CT angiography is the gold-standard test
Thrombus lodged in pulmonary arteries

title: High Altitude Hyperventilation
buzzwords: Hyperventilation in healthy climbers
Low inspired oxygen drives the response
Carotid and aortic chemoreceptors sense hypoxemia
Headache and dizziness above several thousand meters
Reduced FiO2 with normal lungs
Chronic form compensated by bicarbonate excretion
Mountaineer with low PaO2 and low PaCO2
Hypobaric hypoxia-induced respiratory alkalosis

title: Anxiety / Panic Disorder Hyperventilation
buzzwords: Sudden breathlessness with fear of dying
Perioral tingling and hand cramping
Normal PaO2 with low PaCO2
CNS-driven hyperventilation, no lung disease
Episodic, often resolves with reassurance
Carpopedal spasm from acute alkalemia
Most common overall cause of respiratory alkalosis
Nonhypoxemic hyperventilation from panic attack

title: Salicylate (Aspirin) Overdose
buzzwords: Hyperventilation after pill ingestion
Tinnitus and nausea in a toxic patient
Direct stimulation of medullary respiratory center
Early ABG shows isolated low PaCO2
Later evolves into mixed acid-base picture
High anion gap from lactic acid accumulation
Mixed respiratory alkalosis with metabolic acidosis
Aspirin overdose with classic biphasic ABG

title: Pregnancy-Induced Respiratory Alkalosis
buzzwords: Mild chronic dyspnea in a healthy woman
Increased tidal volume without true tachypnea
Hormonally driven bronchodilation
Compensated low PaCO2 over second/third trimester
Serum bicarbonate appropriately decreased
Normal pregnancy ABG finding
Progesterone stimulates the respiratory center
Physiologic chronic respiratory alkalosis of gestation

title: Sepsis-Induced Hyperventilation
buzzwords: Febrile patient with rapid shallow breathing
Tachypnea precedes hemodynamic collapse
Increased metabolic demand drives ventilation
Early ABG shows low PaCO2 and high pH
Cytokine-mediated stimulation of brainstem
May coexist with later lactic acidosis
Nonhypoxemic hyperventilation in systemic infection
Early sepsis presenting as respiratory alkalosis
